words: SAM SCHRAGER: Bovill, you landed up there in the mid '20's when you came.
MARIE FISHER: I had a cousin who lived there. I guess I . applied for
and the job and got it. In those days you would write applications tel-
ling what your qualifications were; what you could teach, and that's
all I did and I got the job.

speaker: SS
words: Where were you living at the time?

speaker: MF
words: My folks were living in Nez Perce, but I was only there during the

speaker: MF
words: summers, so I didn't really live much of anywhere; where my trunk

speaker: MF
words: was is where I lived.

speaker: SS
words: Had you taught before you came to Bovill?

speaker: MF
words: Yes, oh, yes. I must have taught fifteen years, I guess, before I

speaker: MF
words: went to Bovill. No, I hadn't taught that long, I'd taught about

speaker: MF
words: eight years before I went to So I had experience and.good

speaker: MF
words: recommendations, I guess, because I got the job without any trouble.

speaker: MF
words: I taught primary for four years and then they transferred me to fifth

speaker: MF
words: and sixth and then I taught the seventh and eighth. So, I taught

speaker: MF
words: all of them.

speaker: SS
words: Was this all in Bovill or before you came?

speaker: MF
words: No, in Bovill. But I taught rural school and tworoom schools before

speaker: MF
words: I went to Bovill.

speaker: SS
words: You got your teacher training in Lewiston?

speaker: MF
words: Normal.

speaker: SS
words: How long did that take in those days?

speaker: MF
words: Well, when I started out all you had to do was go to summer school

speaker: MF
words: which lasted I think, nine weeks or something like that. And you

speaker: MF
words: got what was called a third grade certificate, and you teach on

speaker: MF
words: that third grade certificate that year then you go back to summer

speaker: MF
words: 4o school and then you go ft second grade and then you'd go back to

speaker: MF
words: summer school the next year and you'd get a first grade and then

speaker: MF
words: you had your certificate. But the people that wanted to go to school

speaker: MF
words: take a whole year at once, they didn't have to go through that.

speaker: MF
words: I couldn't be bothered going the whole year when I could go nine

speaker: MF
words: weeks and get a job.

speaker: MF
words: What was the training itself like? Was it very different then the

speaker: MF
words: way they do the teaching now?

speaker: MF
words: I don't know how they teach now. From the papers I pick up that

speaker: MF
words: the kids drop on the way home, they don't teach like they used to.

speaker: MF
words: These papers that I pick up from the kids that go by an A on

speaker: MF
words: them and I look through 'em and there a whole lot of mistakes on

speaker: MF
words: 'em, they can't spell and they can't write;.big printing. And

speaker: MF
words: when I taught school the kids went to the board and learned to write.

speaker: MF
words: They never did print. But of course, before I quit teaching they

speaker: MF
words: had started printing, but I never bothered with that because I

speaker: MF
words: couldn't see the value of printing first then writing. And we had

speaker: MF
words: to teach Palmer Method of writing in those days.

speaker: MF
words: When you were trained at Lewiston for teaching at the Normal, was

speaker: MF
words: the training very specific? Did they teach you just how to teach

speaker: MF
words: in a classroom, or what?

speaker: MF
words: Yes, they taught the way it should be in a classroom, but after

speaker: MF
words: you got in 4 classroom you had to remember what they told you

speaker: MF
words: These people that were training us were dedicated teachers and all

speaker: MF
words: that, but I don't think that many of them had ever done very much

speaker: MF
words: rural school teaching, where you had eight grades in one room. And

speaker: MF
words: we had special courses in teaching English and special courses in

speaker: MF
words: arithmetic and special teachers to teach us how to teach spelling

speaker: MF
words: and everything else. But after you get out and start doing it you

speaker: MF
words: just forget all that and do it your own way.

speaker: SS
words: What you're saying it was more theory.

speaker: MF
words: Theory, not practice. But I wasn't a bad primary teacher, by

speaker: MF
words: the time Christmas came the kids could pick out different words,

speaker: MF
words: the smart ones. Of course the idiots, morons and^ people on the

speaker: MF
words: curve, they couldn't, but the average kid knew a few words that they

speaker: MF
words: could pick out of the paper. And I never let them learn by the pic

speaker: MF
words: ture on the paper, they had to know the words because I taught

speaker: MF
words: phonics. And they could read pretty good. If you don't believe

speaker: MF
words: it ask Joe Holland, I had his kids in the first grade. And Junior,

speaker: MF
words: his oldest boy, the one that got killed in the army, he could al

speaker: MF
words: most read by Christmas. And by the time school was out he was a

speaker: MF
words: don't know how I ever got kids to learn to read when I didn't teach

speaker: MF
words: phonics, but they must have. But now they're going back to phonics

speaker: MF
words: somebody told me.

speaker: MF
words: Yes, I've heard that, too. Did you have any special methods that

speaker: MF
words: you found worked to get the kids interested in reading? Or was it

speaker: MF
words: strict drills and that sort of thing?

speaker: MF
words: I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: Did you enter any games and that sort of thing?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, sure. The story of the three billy goats. The kids'd read

speaker: MF
words: the story and they'd act out.the three billy goats trying to get

speaker: MF
words: across the bridge that the troll was on. And when you were teaching

speaker: MF
words: manners, you'd have a kid knock at the door and the other kid'd

speaker: MF
words: open the door; "Won't you please come in?" I didn't do that very

speaker: MF
words: often, but once in a while we'd have it- things like that. And,

speaker: MF
words: of course, on Friday afternoons they got a lot of art work and spel

speaker: MF
words: ling matches. Send them up to the board and give them a bunch of

speaker: MF
words: figures and see who could get through it quickv But those days

speaker: MF
words: we used to have contests between our school and Deary, not only in

speaker: MF
words: athletics but in spelling were the only two contests we

speaker: MF
words: had.

speaker: SS
words: Were these frequent get-to-gethers for contests with Deary? Was

speaker: SS
words: it a common thing?

speaker: MF
words: It was for a year or two. It all depended greatly on who the tea

speaker: MF
words: chers were. We finally got to where we didn't go down except they

speaker: MF
words: would have their track meets. But we had a superintendent believed

speaker: MF
words: in developing their mental ability as well as their muscles.

speaker: MF
words: Of course, the Deary superintendent, I don't even remember who he

speaker: MF
words: was, cooperated and of course, it only happened probably once a

speaker: MF
words: year but the kids a lot of fun preparing for it.

speaker: SS
words: Nowdays you really do get the feeling that athletics is more impor

speaker: SS
words: tant than schooling a lot of the time; was it that way then, too?

speaker: MF
words: No. Not in our schools. The idea was to get through all those books

speaker: MF
words: and pass into the next grade and you didn't do that out playing bas

speaker: MF
words: ketball. Of course, I don't know about high school, they were very

speaker: MF
words: basketball minded and of course, everybody liked athletics. They

speaker: MF
words: never had enough kids to have a football team, but they always had

speaker: MF
words: good basketball teams. Girl teams and boy teams, both. And Bovill

speaker: MF
words: had the prettiest girls of the whole Latah County.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard that before. That's so, huh?

speaker: MF
words: Yes, it really is. They were above average for good looks. We only

speaker: MF
words: had one or two fat ones and even the fat ones were nice looking in

speaker: MF
words: the face. I don't have any pictures around of those girls, but they

speaker: MF
words: were good looking. Beautiful girls. And the boys were nice looking,

speaker: MF
words: but the girls were better looking.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds good, that's the way it should be. When you first came to Bovill were

speaker: SS
words: all the primary grades together

speaker: SS
words: as one? Or were they different classes?

speaker: MF
words: They had the first and second grade together, and third and fourth

speaker: MF
words: grade, fifth and sixth, seventh and eighth. There were four grade

speaker: MF
words: teachers and there were, I guess, three or four high school teachers.

speaker: SS
words: Was this an improvement for you as a teacher to be able to just

speaker: SS
words: teach two grades instead of teaching the whole eight in one room?

speaker: MF
words: It was a lot easier, I'll tell you that! You didn't have half the

speaker: MF
words: work to do, and it was more fun, you didn't have to have one kid

speaker: MF
words: teaching another, you could do it all yourself. I enjoyed some of

speaker: MF
words: those one-room schools a lot, too though; especially one out here

speaker: MF
words: out of Lewiston somewhere. Some day I'm going to take the car and

speaker: MF
words: go out and see if I can find that school! You went out to Web bridge

speaker: MF
words: and you went out to that Web post office and. up in those hills

speaker: MF
words: somewhere and there's two roads after you leave Web's store and I

speaker: MF
words: don't know which one I went on.

speaker: SS
words: I'm surprised that you enjoyed the one-room school, I would think

speaker: SS
words: that for a teacher dealing with eight grades at once would just about

speaker: SS
words: be the limit.

speaker: MF
words: But listen- I didn't find it in Bovill the first eight years I

speaker: MF
words: taught there, but if you're the teacher in a one-room school, boy,

speaker: MF
words: you are somebody! The kids respect you; they never try to - the

speaker: MF
words: smartalecky things these kids in town try. Why, if I'd have been

speaker: MF
words: teaching anywhere and the kids'd talk back to me like they do to

speaker: MF
words: these people now, I'd have Because in those days

speaker: MF
words: you could spank kids. Of course, sometimes some of the people didn't

speaker: MF
words: like it very well because their little darlings got spanked, but

speaker: MF
words: they couldn't do anything about it. I wouldn't teach school now

speaker: MF
words: for all the tea in China!

speaker: SS
words: Did each teacher have to develop her own way of dealing with the

speaker: SS
words: problems of kids not paying attention?

speaker: MF
words: Yes. There was never any rebellion in those one-room schools because

speaker: MF
words: school was fun for most of those country kids. They got away from

speaker: MF
words: home and they got in with other kids and they enjoyed school.

speaker: SS
words: Did you feel that you had to be very strict with them?

speaker: MF
words: No. Not the way you do in a town school. I had seven or eight years

speaker: MF
words: experience before I went to Bovill. I'd usually tell 'em the first

speaker: MF
words: morning I went to school I knew all their tricks and that if they'd

speaker: MF
words: be good, I'd be good. If they were going to be mean I knew more

speaker: MF
words: mean ways than they did! And ^ got it across that what I said, that

speaker: MF
words: was going to be it and so I had very little trouble with them; pri

speaker: MF
words: mary grades. I know I got awful macj at the superintendent one time

speaker: MF
words: because he spanked one of my kids, I felt that if the kid needed

speaker: MF
words: spanking I was to spank him! The superintendent did it, he

speaker: MF
words: didn't make any friends or influence any people either. And that

speaker: MF
words: year he left because the kid didn't need a spanking in the first

speaker: MF
words: place, and if he'd needed one I was going to give it to him! But

speaker: MF
words: Ididn't get the chance

speaker: SS
words: I imagine that being a teacher in the school- you were saying be

speaker: SS
words: fore that the first seven or eight years in Bovill, you said there

speaker: SS
words: was no discipline problem?

speaker: MF
words: No, the first seven or eight years when I was teaching in the country

speaker: MF
words: schools there any discipline problems.

speaker: SS
words: Did it take you a matter of time to establish yourself in Bovill

speaker: SS
words: as a—

speaker: MF
words: Disciplinarian?

speaker: SS
words: No, as a person that was going to be there, someone that was- you

speaker: SS
words: say the teacher didn't have the same kind of status in a town as

speaker: SS
words: she did in the country.

speaker: MF
words: Well,the little tiny kids in school in Bovill, they hadn't got to

speaker: MF
words: the point yet, and I don't know whether the upper grades had, but

speaker: MF
words: they could do as they pleased. They came to school just because

speaker: MF
words: school was where they were supposed to be and I told 'em it was just

speaker: MF
words: like their daddy went to work every morning and they went to school,

speaker: MF
words: the same thing. But there wasn't any disciplineA in the primary grades

speaker: MF
words: in Bovill. And I was big and it makes a difference, you know, we

speaker: MF
words: had one little gal come out from New England to teach in Bovill in

speaker: MF
words: high school and she was just a little shrimp, I'll bet she didn't

speaker: MF
words: weigh more than ninety pounds soaking wet, and those kids- I went

speaker: MF
words: down in the basement one day and she was down in the basement crying.

speaker: MF
words: I asked her what was the matter and she said the kids were giving her

speaker: MF
words: a bad time. And I stormed up into that school room and I gave those

speaker: MF
words: kids the damest bawling out you ever saw. I was teaching primary

speaker: MF
words: downstairs, but I'd had these kids and here they were giving her a

speaker: MF
words: bad time. I don't know as it helped any. But anybody that would.

speaker: MF
words: come clear out from Maine to teach a bunch of hoodlums- They weren't

speaker: MF
words: hoodlums, but they -

speaker: SS
words: They were acting like it.

speaker: MF
words: One teacher we had was a little shorty, and she's teaching in Cali

speaker: MF
words: fornia now, about ready to retire, they picked her up one day and

speaker: MF
words: set her in the wastebasket! Just because she was small. Well,

speaker: MF
words: they wouldn't ever have tried that with me! They wouldn't have

speaker: MF
words: dared in the first place because I just looked like they better not

speaker: MF
words: try it, you know! And I think it makes a lot of difference. You

speaker: MF
words: know, some people have a mickey mouse- I'm afraid of you- and I

speaker: MF
words: never looked like I was afraid of 'em. I may have been scared to

speaker: MF
words: death inside, but they didn't know it.

speaker: SS
words: What was it that made you decide to become a teacher in the first

speaker: SS
words: place?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, I don't think I decided. My mother was a teacher. Did Aunt

speaker: MF
words: Mamie teach?

speaker: MF
words: Third person: I don't remember, Marie. I think she did.

speaker: MF
words: Well, anyway, when my mother was raising me the only thing a woman

speaker: MF
words: could do was to teach school or be a nurse. And, of course, they

speaker: MF
words: didn't think nursing was a very— my mother was a very puritanical .

speaker: MF
words: gal, so I didn't think much about it; so I became a teacher«ABut

speaker: MF
words: my cousin, her folks let her be a- take a commercial course. And

speaker: MF
words: I'm better off than she is at that, even if I did teach. Teaching

speaker: MF
words: wasn't bad in those days, but I wouldn't do it again now. Not with

speaker: MF
words: the kid of kids there people are raising.

speaker: SS
words: It seemed like most women, of course, didn't decide to become tea

speaker: SS
words: chers or nurses and most women grew up and got married and that was

speaker: SS
words: it and were homemakers. So in that sense, it always seemed to me

speaker: SS
words: that women who were teachers were different than most women.

speaker: MF
words: Well, maybe I was. The first school I taught was in the country out

speaker: MF
words: of Kamiah. And the man was such a tyrant and so mean, that I

speaker: MF
words: thought, "I'm never going to get married, if that's what it's like!"

speaker: MF
words: Our home, it was perfect and I'd never seen any woman treated

speaker: MF
words: like she was. And I thought, "Nobody's ever going to get the upper

speaker: MF
words: hand of me!" So, I taught for quite a while and finally I got

speaker: MF
words: married.

speaker: SS
words: This man was the one that ran the school?

speaker: MF
words: No. He was just somebody I boarded with, I had to board somewhere.

speaker: MF
words: Boy, that was the meanest man I ever saw.

speaker: SS
words: I know it seems like the men were running things even more than they

speaker: SS
words: do now, in those days. Were teachers supposed to keep their place

speaker: SS
words: and not speak their mind too much when it came to the running of

speaker: SS
words: things? Or were they able to speak out?

speaker: MF
words: I think they could speak out if they wanted to. But I wasn't very

speaker: MF
words: forward or brash. I didn't bother to go to the council meetings

speaker: MF
words: and they didn't have a PTA in Bovill only about one year, about

speaker: MF
words: the time I quit teaching. In Bovill about all the teachers did was

speaker: MF
words: teach school and then go to the parties they were asked to. And

speaker: MF
words: Bovill was quite a partying town; card parties, I mean,

speaker: MF
words: teachers could say what they wanted to, I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: The card parties were frequent, were they? Were there certain groups

speaker: SS
words: that gave them, or people's homes?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, they were card clubs. There was an afternoon bridge club and

speaker: MF
words: a night bridge club and there were about three pinochle clubs and

speaker: MF
words: then every once in a while the different lodges in town would have

speaker: MF
words: a party and get the hall and have the whole town in. Twenty tables,

speaker: MF
words: maybe, something like that. Maybe not twenty tables, ten or twelve

speaker: MF
words: tables, I've forgotten. And then once in a while the different

speaker: MF
words: lodges would decide they'd have a dance. And they had a dance

speaker: MF
words: hall there and there were dances every week. The teachers were al

speaker: MF
words: lowed to go to all that, they were not told they couldn't. Al

speaker: MF
words: though I stayed with one woman said I shouldn't go to dances but I

speaker: MF
words: went anyway.

speaker: MF
words: Were the dances for townspeople mostly?

speaker: MF
words: Yes, they were for everybody in town, neighboring towns, too. Peo

speaker: MF
words: ple would come from Deary and Elk River.

speaker: MF
words: Would lumberjacks go to those, too?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, yeah. The young ones would, because that lived in

speaker: MF
words: Bovill worked in the woods, except a few of the people that had the

speaker: MF
words: stores. All the young fellows worked in the woods and they'd come

speaker: MF
words: to the dances.

speaker: MF
words: I was thinking more of the lumberjacks that weren't local, but lived

speaker: MF
words: in the camps.

speaker: MF
words: Oh, the ones that liked to dance and could get into town, they came.

speaker: MF
words: You say there was a dance just about every week?

speaker: MF
words: Well, I can't remember whether they were that often or not, but they'd

speaker: MF
words: be at least- they'd have them twice a month. And during the Depression

speaker: MF
words: we had dances, I think, about every week down in old buil

speaker: MF
words: ding that had been a church and then a store. Locals, one of 'em

speaker: MF
words: would play a Jew's harp or a mouth , one would play the piano

speaker: MF
words: and one would play the drums. I don't think we had a violin. We

speaker: MF
words: had a lot of fun. Pass the hat to pay the musicians. They'd pro

speaker: MF
words: bably get maybe a dollar a piece; quarters and nickels would be all

speaker: MF
words: they'd et. And we'd wind up at somebody's house for sandwiches

speaker: MF
words: and coffee afterwards.

speaker: MF
words: Did these go way into the night?

speaker: MF
words: No, those days there was a law that you had to stop I think at twelve.

speaker: MF
words: You either had to stop at twelve or one. What was it, Dwight that the

speaker: MF
words: dances used to have to stop at?

speaker: MF
words: Go till one sometimes. Mostly at twelve.

speaker: MF
words: Anyway, it was the law you had to stop. And then we'd go somewhere

speaker: MF
words: and eat and maybe we'd get home at two or three in the morning.

speaker: SS
words: Somebody would put on a feed at their home?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, sandwiches and stuff.

speaker: SS
words: And this was pretty much open to everybody that was at the dance?

speaker: SS
words: That would just be the group, oh, six or eight of us couples would

speaker: SS
words: go. Of course, there'd be probably three or four different people

speaker: SS
words: having refreshments afterward at different homes. About six or

speaker: SS
words: eight couples of us always wound up afterward. And Isuppose there

speaker: SS
words: were six or eight couples wound up some otherwhere else. You

speaker: SS
words: know how it is, you have your own group and you stay in it.

speaker: SS
words: Was your group mostly people- was this after you were married?

speaker: MF
words: Before and after.

speaker: SS
words: Were they people that your husband knew or that-

speaker: SS
words: He knew them, of course, before I did.But I knew them after.

speaker: SS
words: The card playing- Bovill seems a little different than other towns.

speaker: SS
words: I have heard particularly that playing cards was real widespread in

speaker: SS
words: a lot of these towns.

speaker: SS
words: It was in Bovill. I guess there's one card club still running and

speaker: SS
words: I think a three-table club. Our bridge clubs I think were just two

speaker: SS
words: tables because some of them never learned to play very good bridge.

speaker: SS
words: Three of us went over to Moscow to hear that fellow from Oregon

speaker: SS
words: came up to teach us how to play.

speaker: SS
words: Play bridge?

speaker: SS
words: Yes. He's dead now I think and his system has gone out, I've for

speaker: SS
words: gotten what his name was now. He had a system he called horsesense

speaker: SS
words: bridge and he cut it down to just the bare bones of bridge

speaker: SS
words: and it was- and this man Charles Gorham,he's very much like it only

speaker: SS
words: Gorham goes into a - Gorham's book's about that thick. And his

speaker: SS
words: was just about that thick. Just a little paperback book. And he

speaker: SS
words: said everything Gorham said. Gorham puts in a lot more bids like

speaker: SS
words: Blackwood and one no tump means say two clubs and that stuff you

speaker: SS
words: know.

speaker: SS
words: Was that the main game that people played or were there other games

speaker: SS
words: more popular?

speaker: MF
words: Pinochle was more widespread than bridge. But our bridge clubs

speaker: MF
words: lasted forever. As long as I was ' - they were still playing.

speaker: MF
words: And then after four or five of us left town there weren't enough

speaker: MF
words: people left to have a bridge club so it died . And one pinochle

speaker: MF
words: club's still going. I guess, t'was the last time I heard. But

speaker: MF
words: there's a different group in Bovill now. When I am up there I only

speaker: MF
words: see three or four people that were there when I was. Lloyd Hall

speaker: MF
words: is still there and Mrs. Mc Donald and Earl Crane, Sophie Crane,

speaker: MF
words: Earl and Julius Crane and Candelers and Woods and Yangles, that's

speaker: MF
words: about all that's left used to be there.

speaker: SS
words: Was there a lot of church activity too when you first came there?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, yes, when I first went there they had a minister and for two

speaker: MF
words: or three years after I got there they did. Finally the church was

speaker: MF
words: without a minister for quite a while. The Sunday School always

speaker: MF
words: dragged along. I was superintendent of the Sunday School and Sec

speaker: MF
words: retary and name it, I was it in the Sunday School. My husband used

speaker: MF
words: to say- he'd want to go fishing every Sunday- "will, it's your kids

speaker: MF
words: what do you want to do it for?" Somebody had to do it. When I came

speaker: MF
words: down here, Idecided I'd done my work.

speaker: SS
words: Was this Sunday School for one of the churches?

speaker: MF
words: It was Presbyterian. There were two churches the Catholic and the

speaker: MF
words: Presbyterian, and then later on the Nazarenes came in. But they're

speaker: MF
words: gone now, and I noticed they've got a little Baptist deal started.

speaker: SS
words: Mel told me that originally it was a Community Church and everybody

speaker: SS
words: used it, and there was a tiff about Catholics using it and they

speaker: SS
words: wound up building their own.

speaker: MF
words: I never heard of it. Never heard of a tiff between the Catholics,

speaker: MF
words: I thought the trouble was when some woman came in with a tent and

speaker: MF
words: held a revival, and I think her name was Lake, or his name was Lake.

speaker: MF
words: But that had just about died down by the time I'd gotten-ftiere, but

speaker: MF
words: the damage had been done. But I don't think they ever made any trou

speaker: MF
words: ble with the Catholics.

speaker: SS
words: Why was there a problem about the revival?

speaker: MF
words: I don't know, that was way before my time. That was just about for

speaker: MF
words: gotten when I got+here, but it happened early on.

speaker: SS

words: I don't know. There wasn't so awful much drinking in Bovill til

words: after they got beer, and then there got to a lot of people

speaker: MF
words: that drank too much beer for their good. But after beer came on and

speaker: MF
words: then there was some people could get very much inebriated on that.

speaker: MF
words: But there wasn't too much bootleg whiskey around there. Of course,

speaker: MF
words: I wasn't around where I'd know it anyway. I was up there peddling

speaker: MF
words: ABCs all day!

speaker: SS
words: A little better i?r your health than whiskey, that's for sure. Was

speaker: SS
words: there much of a sentiment against drinking among some of the people

speaker: SS
words: in Bovill?

speaker: SS
words: Oh. the good Presbyterians thought it was terrible. They wouldn't

speaker: SS
words: touch a drop, but there were just a few of them.—- believed in living

speaker: SS
words: and letting live. Their own business.

speaker: SS
words: One other person that I always heard as the elite of the town, was

speaker: SS
words: Mrs. Jones. Mrs. T. P. Jones.

speaker: MF
words: Oh, Mrs. T. P. she was elite. Her day had passed by the time I got

speaker: MF
words: there. She was around but she hadn't any following at all. She'd

speaker: MF
words: ceased giving parties or associating with any of the rabble. She

speaker: MF
words: sat there in her house on the hill when she was there. They had an

speaker: MF
words: Eastern Star in Bovill but she didn't join it. I don't know whether

speaker: MF
words: anybody1suggested that she transfer to Bovill or not, but she never

speaker: MF
words: did. But that was before my time. She was supposed to be the Bovill

speaker: MF
words: Queen, but her reign had gone long before I got there. Because when

speaker: MF
words: I got there he'd been in law suits over his ventures and he turned

speaker: MF
words: all his money over to her and when he came downtown to buy anything

speaker: MF
words: he'd have to find out how much it cost and then go back and get the

speaker: MF
words: money from- what was her name? What was her first name. Well, he'd

speaker: MF
words: have to go back and get the money from her.

speaker: SS
words: She doled it out to him?

speaker: MF
words: She doled it out a nickle and dime at a time. AHe'd come down to a

speaker: MF
words: food sale that we'd have and see a pie or something he wanted and

speaker: MF
words: he'd have to go back home and get the money to buy it.

speaker: SS
words: He didn't even have any small change on him?

speaker: MF
words: No, sir! But then she got nuts before she died. She was crazy.

speaker: SS
words: She still stayed in Bovill?

speaker: SS
words: MF She stayed there, I guess, till she died. If you want to the get

speaker: SS
words: the lowdown on Mrs. T. P. Jones, why, one of the women that worked

speaker: SS
words: for her- there were two of 'em- had to go up and take care of her,

speaker: SS
words: you know, because she got pretty rabid. She did something with an

speaker: SS
words: axe one time, tried to chop down the door or get at somebody with a

speaker: SS
words: butcher knife, or something. She lost her— I don't know. I never

speaker: SS
words: was in her house but once.

speaker: SS
words: She had the reputation for really liking to hold onto money before

speaker: SS
words: even T. P. Jones retired. I seemed to have the feeling that I got

speaker: SS
words: from the way people talked about her- that she liked to run things

speaker: SS
words: and run the money and be in charge when she could.

speaker: MF
words: I know she got very much incensed one time- she used to come to churchbecause

speaker: MF
words: some twelve year old kid took communion. And she didn't like

speaker: MF
words: that. Every once in a while she'd buy a new set of song books for

speaker: MF
words: the church. I think it was in 1950 she bought a new song book. And

speaker: MF
words: she'a brought me song books once before. But in those days they

speaker: MF
words: didn't cost too much.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder if her influence waned after T. P. Jones stopped working

speaker: SS
words: for the company, if that had much to do with it.

speaker: MF
words: I think it did because she wouldn't have any whip to crack over any

speaker: MF
words: body. And then too, after he quit working for the company and got

speaker: MF
words: into all these law suits they were gone out of town quite a bit.

speaker: MF
words: I don't know they lived, in Montana, I guess. But I never saw

speaker: MF
words: her at a social function in Bovill. Only place I ever saw her was

speaker: MF
words: in church. Oh, I saw her come down to the post office to get her

speaker: MF
words: mail and stuff like that, but she never went to anything that I ever

speaker: MF
words: went to. Of course, I was teaching school and there could have been

speaker: MF
words: a lot of parties I wasn't at. I'm sure she wasn't there, either.

speaker: SS
words: When you came to Bovill in the late '20's did you consider it a real

speaker: SS
words: lively town?

speaker: SS
words: No, I didn't, I thought it was kind of dull. And Friday, as soon

speaker: SS
words: as school was out I'd get in my little old Model A and I'd head for

speaker: SS
words: St.Maries or Nez Perce or Lewiston or Spokane or somewhere and spent

speaker: SS
words: many weekends there.

speaker: SS
words: What would you dp in those towns?

speaker: SS
words: Two of my friends were teaching up at St. Joe and we d spend the MF

speaker: SS
words: weekend together and my folks lived at Nez Perce then I had friends

speaker: SS
words: down here at Lewiston and relatives in Spokane, so I did not^stay

speaker: SS
words: in town unless I wanted to.

speaker: SS
words: used to have a dance on Hallowe'en that was something. We'd

speaker: SS
words: send to Spokane to some costume company forour costumes, and it was

speaker: SS
words: pretty hard to guess who was in some of those costumes that came down.

speaker: SS
words: What was that theatrical castumft place up there?

speaker: SS
words: I've heard of it.

speaker: SS
words: Apiece? Seems to me it cost us $10 to rent a costume to go to that dance.

speaker: MF
words: Uh-huh. See, they'd ship it down from Spokane and you'd have to

speaker: MF
words: back and I believe the rent was $10.

speaker: SS
words: Did you get to pick one you wanted?

speaker: MF
words: Yeah, you'd write up and tell 'em who you wanted to be. I know one

speaker: MF
words: couple came as two big owls and there was no possible way to tell

speaker: MF
words: who those owls were, because I suppose papermache feathers

speaker: MF
words: pasted on 'em, but they were good owls. And there's be Sir Walter

speaker: MF
words: Raleigh and Queen Elizabeth and Dracula, just name it; that costurner

speaker: MF
words: had it.

speaker: SS
words: Was this in the early days?

speaker: MF
words: That was when I first went there. They only had those, I don't

speaker: MF
words: more than two or three years. Of course, they could have been

speaker: MF
words: more often, I've forgotten. That's a long way back for me to remember.

speaker: SS
words: Fifty years.

speaker: MF
words: And I,

speaker: MF
words: dwell on the past much. 10

speaker: SS
words: That's a good attitude. And this transportation in those days, in the Model A,

speaker: SS
words: how

speaker: SS
words: good were the roads then?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, they were gravelled. And they weren't very wide. I know the

speaker: MF
words: first time I drove to St. Maries, the girls came down on the train

speaker: MF
words: from St. show me how to get up there. So, I drove back to

speaker: MF
words: St. Joe, and I was a pretty good driver and I guess I must have

speaker: MF
words: driven pretty fast and had those girls scared pea green, and they

speaker: MF
words: told me I sure came fast the St. Joe River was right down

speaker: MF
words: there, and when I drove back Sunday and saw that road, it was just

speaker: MF
words: two wheel tracks wide and I didn't meet anybody I guess, but if I

speaker: MF
words: did I met 'em soon enough to get by but it sure scared me going back.

speaker: MF
words: In those days you could drive down from St. Maries toward Emida, some

speaker: MF
words: times I'd be pretty sleepy and I'd pull off to the side of the road

speaker: MF
words: and try to sleep. And one time when I was going down at night I

speaker: MF
words: could hear those- I thought it was a hyena howling- oh a bobcat, I

speaker: MF
words: guess it was- they cry like women, you know, like a baby.

speaker: SS
words: The cougars do that. The cougars.

speaker: MF
words: That's what I was trying to say. I've heard cougars several times.

speaker: SS
words: You'd just pull off to the side of the road and sleep for a while?

speaker: MF
words: Well, that would be in the daytime when it would be hot. But when

speaker: MF
words: I'd be caught up there after dark, I'd keep right on going! I

speaker: MF
words: wouldn t think of stopping a car and taking a nap now. All the freaks

speaker: MF
words: running around.

speaker: SS
words: In winter, didn't Bovill pretty much put its cars up and forget

speaker: SS
words: about 'em?

speaker: MF
words: I'll say they did. We had a very bad strip of road they called

speaker: MF
words: Hog Meadows. And if you got through Hog Meadows and got into town

speaker: MF
words: you never went out. One year I came to Nez Perce for Christmas and

speaker: MF
words: I left my car in a snowbank over there by Nez Perce, so other people

speaker: MF
words: had deep snow, too. But, we'd put our cars up in the winter and they'd

speaker: MF
words: stay there till spring. And Hubert Hall was always the first one in

speaker: MF
words: his car- over the hill to Clarkia. Of course, the rest of us would

speaker: MF
words: wait two or three weeks before we'd it.

speaker: SS
words: What was the idea?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, just to be the first. Wasn't any point in it at all.

speaker: SS
words: Did things change dramatically the Depression hit Bovill? Was

speaker: SS
words: there sudden change or was it gradual; and what was the change?

speaker: MF
words: I don't remember about that. But it sure was a great leveler. Only

speaker: MF
words: two or three people had any money during the Depression, the rest of

speaker: MF
words: us scratched along. The Depression was kind of bad because that

speaker: MF
words: bank in Bovill took the people's checks the night before they put out

speaker: MF
words: the sign the next morning the bank was closed. That was a dirty

speaker: MF
words: trick, because they knew that- I think it was closed the 2nd of Feb

speaker: MF
words: ruary. It was Ground Hog's Day. And they took the fellows' paychecks

speaker: MF
words: and that was it. I think. got about ten cents on the dollar back.

speaker: MF
words: But I didn't have much in there because I was sitting down at the

speaker: MF
words: restaurant one day having my lunch and there were two fellows - I

speaker: MF
words: was eating at the counter and they were down.farther, and I heard 'em

speaker: MF
words: talking about the banks were closing. So I called up- Erwin

speaker: MF
words: and I told him that- what I'd heard at the restaurant

speaker: MF
words: they were talking kind of low but I had good ears- so they didn't

speaker: MF
words: catch me with much money in the bank. Those days you had stockings

speaker: MF
words: that had a seam up the back and this big thing that folded over the

speaker: MF
words: top where you fastened your garter had a slit in it about that wide

speaker: MF
words: and I kept my money in the top of my sock! So, this idea of money

speaker: MF
words: in your sock was really true, because if I hadn't had my money in my

speaker: MF
words: sock I'd a been in a bad way.

speaker: SS
words: You pulled it out of the bank right away then?

speaker: SS
words: I always kept pretty well spent up in paying my rent and my food

speaker: SS
words: and buy some clothes and gas and stuff, and you see it was right af-

speaker: MF
words: after Christmas, it was February when it closed, so I didn't have much

speaker: MF
words: it in. But I hadn't any in- well, I may have had some in, but I didn't

speaker: MF
words: have a great deal. But a lot of the people had their money down in

speaker: MF
words: bank and went down and got it out. But I guess it didn't close,

speaker: SS
words: What happened once the Depression hit and the company pulled the pin

speaker: SS
words: like they say, and the men were out of work?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, nothing. That's when they had- I guess it was Red Cross flour

speaker: MF
words: and Red Cross sugar and beans and I think stuff, like that, down at

speaker: MF
words: arker's store and the people that were hard up could go down there

speaker: MF
words: and get it, people with large families, would go down and get some.

speaker: MF
words: And Mr. Nogle was the chief of the Potlatch in town, and he would

speaker: MF
words: hire some of the men to shovel the sidewalks and things like that,

speaker: MF
words: so they had a little bit of money. And what else did they do? They

speaker: MF
words: had WPA then, too. And WPA I think put in some new water pipes, or

speaker: MF
words: something.

speaker: SS
words: They did some road building, I think, that's what somebody told me.

speaker: MF
words: And then they had those CCC camps, that was a big help then, too.

speaker: MF
words: Brought all those kids out of the East and sent our kids- I don't

speaker: MF
words: know where the local kids went. Some of 'em worked out in the camps

speaker: MF
words: around Bovill.

speaker: MF
words: The presence of those kids from the East make itself felt in

speaker: MF
words: the town?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, yes, they'd be up at the dances and dance with all the girls.

speaker: MF
words: One of the girls married one of 'em; went back to New Jersey and

speaker: MF
words: I visited there one time,

speaker: MF
words: lived. She died this summer so I won't go back. And one or two of

speaker: MF
words: the CCs never went back East they stayedfhere. One of them married

speaker: MF
words: one of the Sanderson girls. The one that runs p. restaurant, I

speaker: MF
words: can't think what her name is- what his name was. I think he came

speaker: MF
words: in with the CCs. fc helped the economy quite a bit.

speaker: MF
words: Do you think there was much actual suffering, and real deprivation

speaker: MF
words: during those days for some of the people in town?

speaker: MF
words: I don't think there was in Bovill because if anyone heard of anyone

speaker: MF
words: being out of food there would be food taken into them. There was

speaker: MF
words: one family reported to be sleeping on the floor and they took in bed

speaker: MF
words: ding and mattresses and stuff. I don't know why they should have

speaker: MF
words: been sleeping on the floor if they were there before the Depression

speaker: MF
words: started. And one of our local men got books for the kids that couldn't

speaker: MF
words: afford them, and somebody else bought overshoes for one family with

speaker: MF
words: eight kids. They helped each other out. Then they got free flour

speaker: MF
words: and stuff. And then if they could get bullets they could go out and

speaker: MF
words: get deer. They could get venison and I don't know whether the elk

speaker: MF
words: were plentiful or not. So I think most people managed to

speaker: MF
words: have meat. And a lot of people cut wood; sold it to get extra dollars

speaker: MF
words: because there were lots of woodstoves. then.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the togetherness of the community was a lot stron

speaker: SS
words: ger then during the Depression than it was before, or was it always

speaker: SS
words: there?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, I think it was probably there, but it wasn't so evident. Be

speaker: MF
words: cause it seems to me all of us were doing the same things after the

speaker: MF
words: Depression as we were before, only we probably didn't do them in quite

speaker: MF
words: such a grand manner. If we had a party we probably had a very light

speaker: MF
words: lunch and if it hadn't been a depression we'd probably ha^l nuts and

speaker: MF
words: candy and whipped cream and sandwiches and dessert and everything.

speaker: MF
words: K Bovill used to have . plays, too. Community plays. We

speaker: MF
words: had lots of fun with those. They never tried any Shakespeare or

speaker: MF
words: anything like that, it'd usually be a farce of some sort.^, Sometimes

speaker: MF
words: they'd take plays- I think they took it to Deary and other places.

speaker: MF
words: I wasn't in that one. But they put on a play once called The Patsy.

speaker: MF
words: And then I coached one called, Slick from Punkin Creek." And

speaker: MF
words: that was a farce, but it was a lot of fun and well received. And

speaker: MF
words: they put on "The Old Maids' Convention" once. Bunch of us women

speaker: MF
words: put on the on a play. I think there were seven characters.

speaker: MF
words: Four of them were women and three were men and I was one of the men

speaker: MF
words: and two of the other women were men. It was a lot of fun. A dog had

speaker: MF
words: to come in on the scene, another time a gal had to open up a bottle

speaker: MF
words: of something,^ shak£n, it you know and when she opened it it

speaker: MF
words: spewed all over everybody.

speaker: SS
words: Was that supposed to happen?

speaker: SS
words: M^ No. It was just supposed to be opened, I think it was grapejuice

speaker: SS
words: or something . And another time they had- one of

speaker: SS
words: those plays they had to have a lunch and they spiked the stuff

speaker: SS
words: "these actors" That was kind of funny.

speaker: SS
words: During the play?

speaker: MF
words: Uh-huh. They didn't do it at rehearsal but they did it the main

speaker: MF
words: night. That was fun,

speaker: SS
words: Seems like the plays were much more important than in a lot of com

speaker: SS
words: munities. I didn't know they had so many.

speaker: MF
words: They had 'em by spurts, you know. There was one fellow in town, this

speaker: MF
words: Parker had been an actor and he had written radio plays,

speaker: MF
words: I think they were on KGO, and he'd been on a tour to the Orient at

speaker: MF
words: the time of the Japanese earthquake with a traveling troupe. So

speaker: MF
words: the winters he'd be in town, why, there'd always be a play because

speaker: MF
words: he'd see to it. And then maybe the next year I'd direct or somebody

speaker: MF
words: else would.

speaker: SS
words: What did he do in town?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, he'd just come- he was a ne'er-do-well. He would just come to

speaker: MF
words: town and put in his time until he went off to do something else.

speaker: MF
words: I don't know how he made his living. But he had great promise but

speaker: MF
words: amounted to nothing, a spoiled mama's boy.

speaker: SS
words: He liked Bovill though?

speaker: MF
words: Yes. It was a good place to rest up and he had fun putting on these

speaker: MF
words: plays. I know.one he put on was "The Patsy" and I can't remember

speaker: MF
words: what the other was.

speaker: SS
words: Did he spend other time in big cities and that kind of thing?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, yes. He would be around San Francisco and Portland. I know one

speaker: MF
words: night I heard one of the plays that he had written over KGO myself,

speaker: MF
words: so I know he did it, besides that we had clippings and stuff so we

speaker: SS
words: knew he was an actor. Good looking. He was a handsome brute. I

speaker: MF
words: don't know why he picked Bovill as a place to hang out. Well, his

speaker: MF
words: parents lived there. His parents had the store. He used to go to

speaker: MF
words: the University of Idaho years and years ago.

speaker: SS
words: Was this the E. K. Parker store.

speaker: MF
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: What ever happened to the man?

speaker: MF
words: He finally died and he's burried up there in the Moscow cemetery.

speaker: SS
words: Were there movies regularly shown in Bovill?

speaker: MF
words: Oh, yes. Mr. Denevan had a picture show and it ran every night for

speaker: MF
words: years.

speaker: SS
words: This is Lucille Denevan's husband?

speaker: MF
words: Father. Father-in-law. And I'm sure there was a show every night.

speaker: MF
words: They would have these serials that continued, you know, don't think

speaker: MF
words: it was called the Perils of Pauline; but a long time ago they'd have

speaker: MF
words: these shows where the heroine would be tied on the tracks and the

speaker: MF
words: engine'd be coming down to run over her and she'd be saved at the

speaker: MF
words: last minute. And they had those things, I think it was every Sunday

speaker: MF
words: night and a feature besides. And then there used to be theatrih

speaker: MF
words: cal troupes come through town and rent the hall and have plays

speaker: MF
words: too.

speaker: SS
words: Did they call that Chautauques?

speaker: MF
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: Something else.

speaker: MF
words: They had Chautauques in Bovill, too, but the Chautauques was put on

speaker: MF
words: in a tent. They'd always bring their own tent. But these were tra

speaker: MF
words: veling troupes. The Malon Players; did you ever hear od them?

speaker: SS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: MF
words: Well, they used to come to Bovill all the time. I think they were

speaker: MF
words: out of Spokane, weren't they?

speaker: SS
words: I don't know, but I have heard the name.

speaker: MF
words: And it seems to me that acted with the Malon Players a while,

speaker: MF
words: but he may not have. And they would make Bovill, and that went on

speaker: MF
words: for several years and I don't know why they quit coming. I guess

speaker: MF
words: the beer parlors opened up and the people^drink beer.

speaker: SS
words: You say the town was so dead but it sounds like there was some things

speaker: SS
words: going on.

speaker: MF
words: Yeah, there was, I guess. But then that was over a period of many

speaker: MF
words: years, you know. Just a day at a.time^didn't seem very exciting.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that Bovill was a very close town? That people that

speaker: SS
words: lived in the town were very close to each other as compared, let's

speaker: SS
words: say, to some of the farming towns or other places?

speaker: MF
words: I don't know. I never lived in a farming town. Well, I guess I

speaker: MF
words: did when I was a little kid but I don't remember anything about that,

speaker: MF
words: said Bovill was a^fful cliquey, and I guess that means close,

speaker: MF
words: doesn't it?

speaker: SS
words: That's close but that's not close for everybody.

speaker: SS
words: There was a great deal more closeness among people in those days

speaker: SS
words: then there is nowdays- I don't know whether or not that's really

speaker: SS
words: true. Maybe people idealize the past, but it does se%m-

speaker: MF
words: They really were a bit more friendly. Take in Lewiston; if anybody

speaker: MF
words: dies across the alley, I won't know it. And last night the police

speaker: MF
words: car was over on that corner for a long, long time; I don't know why

speaker: MF
words: they were there. Now, in a small town we'd have been out and we'd

speaker: MF
words: have known why, but here in a town this size, you don't pay any at

speaker: MF
words: tention to it. But if anything like that had lattppened in a town

speaker: MF
words: of 500 or 600 the whole town would have been gathered around to see

speaker: MF
words: what was happening. And I think the urban community is self-centered.

speaker: SS
words: What do you mean?

speaker: MF
words: I mean, they're all for "me". Phooey, on you! Time is going so fast

speaker: MF
words: and people have got so much to do and so little time to do it in,

speaker: MF
words: that they don't consider the other person much any more. When we

speaker: MF
words: lived at a slower pace, we were more interested in our fellow man.

speaker: MF
words: And I think if we can't run our cars and can't keep our houses warm

speaker: MF
words: the people are going to get a lot more friendly, too. Go where some

speaker: MF
words: body's got a good fireplace.

speaker: SS
words: Are you suggesting by that that hard times help bring people together?

speaker: MF
words: you bet you! Surely do! It'll either bring them together or the

speaker: MF
words: have-nots will get something that the haves have. And it's either

speaker: MF
words: got to bring them together or bring revolution, because it's not fair

speaker: MF
words: for some people to have it all and the rest of us just sit around

speaker: MF
words: and shiver.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think the attitude of people in Bovill was towards the

speaker: SS
words: Potlatch Lumber Company?

speaker: MF
words: I was just talking with Mrs. Krier a week or two ago and the attitude

speaker: MF
words: of the Bovill people toward the Potlatch Lumber Company in the early

speaker: MF
words: days was it was"Papa Potlatch" or "Uncle Potlatch". Before they became Weyerhauser

speaker: MF
words: they were just community operation. But after they became

speaker: MF
words: Weyerhaueser a lot of the closeness disappeared. You know this lady

speaker: MF
words: said the other day she just hated to hear people say mean things about

speaker: MF
words: the lumber company. That doesn't bother me .any. Because the

speaker: MF
words: Potlatch used to send turkeys to the people at Christmastime, and

speaker: MF

words: turn 'm over to their wives and that's the last they saw of it. We

speaker: MF
words: didn't have any feeling of being under anybody's thumb up there, be

words: cause very few men knew whether they had five cents or five thousand

speaker: MF
words: in the bank. Of course, they worked, see, and the women'd have to

speaker: MF
words: take the check to the bank and cash it, because they wouldn't lay

words: right off the bat.

words: me!

words: But I don't think I did. I could get along with people.

words: sixteen than I was when I was twenty-five, I guess. They're smart.

words: I always go to say immoral, but that wasn't the word. What effect

words: that's going to have twenty years from now. One gal was telling

words: the other day, "My goodness, they way these kids were having kids

words: that grow up and marry their own brothers and sisters." What kind

words: of a race would that be? Well, I don't know, Cleopatra was married

words: to her brother. She made a name for herself! Even if she did com

words: mit suicide with that asp. And so, I don't know.

words: them in those days? For one thing, it seems to me, if teaching and

words: Well, I think it's theii? own fault- it's all in their motivation,

speaker: MF

words: and have a bunch of dirty-nosed kids, but if they have any ambition

words: well, maybe the next place you'd have a better offer! And that was

speaker: MF
words: the way it was. . you went to teach, somebody - they just

words: couldn't live without you.  And then I'd go to the next place and

words: nothing like shopping around, you know. And that Old Man Bowles,

words: of a predicament! And then after you get to be about twenty-five

words: years old you look around at the men and you look 'em over and you

words: trying to take you out - and I didn't go for that kind of stuff.

speaker: SS
words: when you were there? Was there any politics going on around town

speaker: SS
words: around elections?

speaker: MF
words: Oh,not very much, because Bovill was practically all Democrats. I

speaker: MF
words: think there were just three or four or five families that were Re

speaker: MF
words: publican.

speaker: MF

speaker: MF
words: elite, I think were Republican and I think the working man I think

speaker: MF

words: My husband was precinct committeeman or something. He'd always say,

speaker: MF
words: Well, no matter what the President is going to be, I'll have to go

speaker: MF
words: to work in the morning anyway!" So they didn't bother so much.

words: Was he a Democrat?

speaker: MF
words: I couldn't say which he was. He was on the Republican list as Re

speaker: MF
words: publican Precinct Chairman, but he never did anything about it. He

speaker: MF
words: just kind of left politics to the people that wanted to be politicians.

speaker: SS
words: And there were few of those?

speaker: MF
words: that Negro Congresswoman. And I thought how true most of it was.

speaker: MF
words: She's a Democrat. But when she described those meetings in the Senate

speaker: MF
words: Building, you know, in the Capitol I thought, well, gosh, she saw exactly

speaker: MF
words: what I saw. Nobody listened to anybody; feet on the desks, somebody

speaker: MF
words: reading a newspaper and somebody down there yapping and nobody lis

speaker: MF
words: tening to them. And she said that's what they were doing now, yet,

speaker: MF
words: and this book was written I think in '72.

speaker: SS
words: When did you see that?

speaker: MF
words: When I was in Washington, D. C. one time back in the '60's.

words: here; couldn't learn. Those were the only problem kids I had. They

speaker: MS

speaker: MS

speaker: MS
words: If they'd tell 'em they weren't nervous and what was going on was

speaker: MS
words: alright, why, I think the kids would straighten up and fly right.

speaker: MS

speaker: MS

speaker: MS
words: hasn't a nerve in his body. Because he was just plain mean. He

speaker: MF
words: Vicious?

words: than when I see him.
